message:
feat(core): migrate fd_budget + priority to lock-free PaneMap (wa-5d30)

Replace RwLock<HashMap<u64, T>> with sharded PaneMap<T> in fd_budget.rs
and priority.rs for reduced lock contention on hot paths. Add five new
PaneMap helper methods: values(), entries(), insert_if_absent(),
for_each_mut(), map_all_mut().

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

-{"id":"wa-1bznu","title":"Phase 4: Replace tokio task spawning with asupersync structured concurrency","description":"# Replace tokio task spawning with asupersync structured concurrency

## Goal
Replace all tokio::spawn/JoinHandle/JoinSet with asupersync's structured concurrency (Scope/region). This bead establishes the patterns; per-module application is done in module-specific beads.

## Key paradigm shift
Tokio allows detached tasks (fire-and-forget). Asupersync enforces structured concurrency — every task belongs to a Scope, and the scope waits for all children before exiting.

## Scope (what this bead covers)
- Establish cx.region() / scope.spawn() patterns
- Define how to replace JoinHandle (scope child handle)
- Define how to replace JoinSet (region with multiple spawns + semaphore for bounding)
- Document fire-and-forget redesign strategies:
  - Option A: Attach to long-lived application scope
  - Option B: Dedicated background region for app lifetime
  - Option C: Refactor to not need detached tasks
- Create helper utilities for common patterns (bounded spawn, spawn-with-timeout)

## What this does NOT cover
- select! macro replacement — that's per-module work because each module has different select! branches. Module beads (wa-p48pw, wa-1m7nk, wa-16hou, etc.) handle their own select! migration using asupersync::combinator::select/race.

## Patterns to establish
```rust
// Bounded concurrent work (replaces JoinSet with max)
pub async fn spawn_bounded<F, T>(
    cx: &mut Cx, max: usize, tasks: Vec<F>
) -> Outcome<Vec<T>, Error>
where F: FnOnce(&mut Cx) -> Fut...
{
    cx.region(|scope| async {
        let sem = Semaphore::new(max);
        for task in tasks {
            let permit = sem.acquire(cx).await?;
            scope.spawn(move |cx| async move {
                let _p = permit;
                task(cx).await
            });
        }
    }).await
}
```

## Unit tests
- Test: scope.spawn() runs task to completion
- Test: scope exit waits for all children
- Test: bounded spawn respects concurrency limit
- Test: cancellation propagates to all scope children
- Test: nested regions work correctly
- All tests use LabRuntime with seed-locked scheduling

## Acceptance criteria
- Structured concurrency patterns documented and tested
- Helper utilities for bounded spawn, spawn-with-timeout
- Fire-and-forget redesign strategy decided
- 5+ LabRuntime tests for the patterns

## LabRuntime DPOR
- **Structured concurrency testing with DPOR**: Use LabRuntime schedule exploration (DPOR) to exhaustively test structured concurrency guarantees:
  - Verify scope exit always waits for ALL children under every possible interleaving
  - Verify cancellation propagates to all scope children regardless of scheduling order
  - Verify nested region teardown ordering (inner scopes complete before outer)
  - Verify bounded spawn never exceeds concurrency limit under any schedule

## Benchmark requirements
- **Criterion benchmarks for task spawn overhead**: Add `benches/task_spawn.rs` measuring:
  - scope.spawn() latency for a no-op task
  - Region creation + N spawns + join overhead for N=1,10,100,1000
  - Comparison with tokio::spawn for equivalent workloads
  - Structured concurrency overhead: region vs flat spawn

## Cross-references
- See wa-brc7d.5 (mux crate in asupersync FrankenTerm crates epic) — the mux crate is the heaviest user of task spawning for pane output subscriptions and background polling.  {"id":"wa-1c2u","title":"Smart Scrollback with Importance Weighting","description":"## Goal
Replace WezTerm's fixed scrollback limit with importance-weighted scrollback that retains high-value output (errors, tool results, compilation output) longer than low-value output (blank lines, repeated prompts), reducing memory usage while preserving the content that matters.

## Background & Motivation
WezTerm's scrollback is a fixed-size ring buffer (default 3,500 lines per pane). With 50 panes, that's 175,000 lines of terminal cell data in memory. But scrollback content varies enormously in value:
- High value: error messages, compilation output, test results, tool_use boundaries
- Low value: blank lines, repeated prompts, ANSI cursor movements, progress bars

Fixed limits waste memory on low-value content while potentially evicting high-value content.

## Technical Design

### Importance Scoring
```rust
pub struct ScrollbackLine {
    pub cells: Vec<Cell>,
    pub importance: f64,  // 0.0 (garbage) to 1.0 (critical)
    pub timestamp: Instant,
}

fn score_line(line: &str, context: &PaneContext) -> f64 {
    let mut score = 0.3;  // baseline

    // High value indicators
    if patterns::is_error(line) { score += 0.4; }
    if patterns::is_tool_boundary(line) { score += 0.3; }
    if patterns::is_compilation_output(line) { score += 0.2; }
    if patterns::is_test_result(line) { score += 0.3; }

    // Low value indicators
    if line.trim().is_empty() { score -= 0.2; }
    if patterns::is_progress_bar(line) { score -= 0.3; }
    if patterns::is_ansi_only(line) { score -= 0.3; }

    score.clamp(0.0, 1.0)
}
```

### Eviction Policy
Instead of FIFO eviction, use importance-weighted eviction:
```rust
fn evict_line(scrollback: &mut VecDeque<ScrollbackLine>) -> Option<ScrollbackLine> {
    // Find lowest importance line in oldest 25% of scrollback
    let quarter = scrollback.len() / 4;
    let (min_idx, _) = scrollback.iter()
        .take(quarter)
        .enumerate()
        .min_by(|(_, a), (_, b)| a.importance.partial_cmp(&b.importance).unwrap())
        .unwrap();
    scrollback.remove(min_idx)
}
```

### Memory Budget
Instead of line count, use byte budget per pane:
```rust
pub struct ScrollbackConfig {
    pub byte_budget_per_pane: usize,  // default: 2MB
    pub min_lines: usize,             // always keep at least this many (500)
    pub max_lines: usize,             // hard cap (10,000)
    pub importance_threshold: f64,    // never evict above this (0.8)
}
```

### Integration with patterns.rs
Reuses existing pattern detection from patterns.rs (3-stage Bloom→Aho-Corasick→Regex) for line classification. The importance scorer is a thin wrapper over existing pattern rules.

### Implementation Location
- Modify: FrankenTerm fork's terminal/src/terminalstate/mod.rs (scrollback management)
- Integration: patterns.rs provides line classification
- Config: scrollback section in wa-core config

## Existing Code References
- patterns.rs: 100+ pattern rules, 3-stage matching (reuse for line classification)
- FrankenTerm terminal crate: scrollback ring buffer (modify eviction policy)

## Configuration
```toml
[scrollback]
mode = "importance_weighted"  # "fixed" | "importance_weighted"
byte_budget_per_pane_mb = 2
min_lines = 500
max_lines = 10000
importance_threshold = 0.8    # Never evict lines above this
```

## Dependencies
- FrankenTerm fork (bd-20fw): modifying terminal scrollback internals
- patterns.rs: reuse pattern rules for importance scoring
- Enhances memory reduction (wa-3kxe): less scrollback memory per pane

## Acceptance Criteria
- Importance scoring for each scrollback line
- Weighted eviction: low-value lines evicted before high-value
- Byte budget instead of line count
- Integration with existing pattern rules
- Configurable thresholds and budgets
- Unit tests: synthetic scrollback, verify high-value retention
- Memory benchmark: RSS reduction vs fixed scrollback under 50-pane load

## Benchmark Requirements
- **criterion benchmarks** with target `benches/importance_scoring.rs`:
  - `score_line_latency`: measure time to compute importance score for a single line. Target: <1us per line (must be fast enough for inline scoring during terminal output).
  - `score_batch_throughput`: measure scoring throughput for 10,000 lines of mixed content. Target: >1M lines/sec.
  - `eviction_selection`: measure time to find the lowest-importance line in the oldest 25% of a 10,000-line scrollback. Target: <50us per eviction decision.
  - `byte_budget_enforcement`: measure overhead of byte-budget tracking vs simple line-count tracking. Target: <5% overhead.

## Property-Based Testing (proptest)
- **Scoring monotonicity**: if line A matches strictly more high-value indicators than line B (and no more low-value indicators), then `score(A) >= score(B)`. More signals of importance always result in equal or higher scores.
- **Score range invariant**: for any input string, `score_line()` returns a value in [0.0, 1.0]. No combination of pattern matches can produce a score outside this range.
- **Eviction ordering**: lines with importance >= `importance_threshold` are never evicted while lines with importance < `importance_threshold` exist in the scrollback. The threshold is a hard floor.
- **Budget compliance**: after any sequence of insertions and evictions, `total_bytes <= byte_budget_per_pane` (within one line's tolerance for the last insertion that triggered eviction).

## Cross-References
- **wa-3r5e** (Scrollback memory pressure mitigation): wa-3r5e makes pane-level decisions about how aggressively to trim scrollback under memory pressure, while wa-1c2u makes line-level decisions about which lines to keep. Under pressure, wa-3r5e reduces the byte budget, and wa-1c2u uses importance scoring to decide which lines survive within that reduced budget.
- **wa-283h4.8** (Entropy-aware scheduling): entropy measurements can inform importance scoring — lines with high information entropy (novel content) should score higher than low-entropy lines (repeated patterns). This provides a principled, information-theoretic foundation for the heuristic scoring rules.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-09T22:45:14.755885Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T19:48:48.903634Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-1c2u","depends_on_id":"wa-3kxe","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-09T22:45:32.328834Z","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"wa-1c2u","depends_on_id":"wa-ixt4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T23:16:56.597823Z","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"wa-1c2u","depends_on_id":"wa-20fw","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T23:16:56.597823Z","created_by":"jemanuel"}]}

 {"id":"wa-1ta8j","title":"Proactive pane lifecycle management — zombie reaping, stuck detection, age-based cleanup","description":"# Proactive Pane Lifecycle Management

## Skills: /extreme-software-optimization, /idea-wizard

## Problem
At 200+ panes, abandoned/stuck agents accumulate silently. A 54-hour agent consuming 118GB RAM was observed in production. Zombie processes consume process table slots. Stuck test processes (12+ hours) waste CPU.

## Solution: Activity-Based Lifecycle Engine

**NOTE**: This bead CONSUMES process_triage (wa-2zywl) as a library — it does NOT reimplement zombie reaping or stuck detection. process_triage provides the ProcessClassifier/TriageEngine/WhackAMoleDetector; this bead adds the FrankenTerm-specific health classification and pane-aware policies on top.

### Pane Health Classification (from /system-performance-remediation)
| Age | CPU% | Status | Action |
|-----|------|--------|--------|
| <4h | >10% | Active | Protect |
| <4h | <2% | Thinking | Protect |
| 4-16h | >5% | Working | Check children |
| 4-16h | <2% | Possibly stuck | Flag for review |
| 16-24h | any | Likely stuck | Kill if only MCP children |
| >24h | any | Abandoned | Kill immediately |

### Components to build

1. **PaneHealthMonitor**: Background task sampling per-pane CPU%, memory, child count, age
   - Sample every 30s (configurable)
   - Store in ring buffer for trend analysis
   - Expose via MCP tool and CLI
   - **macOS**: Use libproc/proc_pidinfo instead of /proc/[pid]/stat

2. **PaneTriagePolicy**: Wraps process_triage TriageEngine with pane-aware context
   - Maps pane health classification to process_triage kill hierarchy
   - Adds FrankenTerm-specific categories: "agent thinking" vs "agent stuck"
   - Per-pane policy overrides via configuration

3. **AgeReaper**: Configurable age-based cleanup policies
   - Default: warn at 16h, kill at 24h
   - Per-pane overrides via configuration
   - Graceful shutdown: SIGTERM, wait 30s, SIGKILL

4. **Resource pressure response**: When PSI avg10 > 30% (Linux) or CPU load > 80% (macOS)
   - Renice oldest idle panes to nice=19, ionice class 3 (idle)
   - Pause non-essential captures
   - Warn user via notification

## Formal requirement
The stuck detector whack-a-mole logic lives in process_triage (wa-2zywl) with formal verification. This bead only needs to verify that PaneTriagePolicy correctly maps pane context to process_triage categories.

## Tests
- Simulate 200 panes with various ages/CPU profiles, verify correct classification
- Test PaneTriagePolicy maps to process_triage categories correctly
- Test age reaper respects graceful shutdown
- Load test: 200 pane health checks in <100ms total
- **Use criterion benchmarks for health check latency**

## Acceptance criteria
- Health classification matches the table above
- Delegates zombie/stuck/whack-a-mole to process_triage library
- Age reaper respects graceful shutdown
- CPU pressure triggers renice within 5s
- Works on both Linux (/proc) and macOS (libproc)
- All under /extreme-software-optimization methodology: profile before/after","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-10T16:10:51.270912Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-11T00:08:24.676026-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-11T00:08:24.676026-05:00","close_reason":"Closed","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-1ta8j","depends_on_id":"wa-2zywl","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T16:14:17.414441Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"},{"issue_id":"wa-1ta8j","depends_on_id":"wa-a0owq","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T16:14:17.553827Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"}]}
 {"id":"wa-1u55z","title":"Replace TCP networking and TLS (tokio-rustls → asupersync)","description":"# Replace TCP networking and TLS

## Background
TCP and TLS are used by feature-gated modules: web server (web.rs), distributed mode (distributed.rs), and HTTP clients (reqwest).

## Scope

### TCP (web.rs)
- TcpListener for the HTTP API server
- Bound to configurable port, serves /health, /panes, /events, /search, etc.
- Currently uses fastapi::TcpServer wrapper around tokio TcpListener

### TLS (distributed.rs)
- tokio-rustls 0.26 for mTLS between distributed nodes
- Certificate and private key loading
- TLS 1.2+ configuration
- Client and server TLS config

### HTTP Client (reqwest)
- reqwest 0.12.12 with rustls-tls feature
- Used for outbound API calls
- Must be replaced with asupersync::http client

## Migration
```rust
// TCP Before
use tokio::net::TcpListener;
let listener = TcpListener::bind(addr).await?;

// TCP After
use asupersync::net::TcpListener;
let listener = TcpListener::bind(cx, addr).await?;

// TLS Before
use tokio_rustls::TlsConnector;

// TLS After
use asupersync::tls::TlsConnector;  // rustls-based, no tokio dep

// HTTP Before
let resp = reqwest::get(url).await?;

// HTTP After
let client = asupersync::http::Client::new();
let resp = client.get(cx, url).await?;
```

## Note on FastAPI/FastMCP
These vendored frameworks are built on tokio. They will need either:
1. Porting to asupersync (significant effort)
2. Replacing with asupersync-native HTTP/MCP server (if available)
3. Running in a compatibility shim (last resort)

This bead covers the direct TCP/TLS replacement. FastAPI/FastMCP refactoring is tracked separately.

## Acceptance criteria
- TCP server binds and accepts connections via asupersync
- TLS handshake works with asupersync TLS
- HTTP client makes successful outbound requests
- reqwest dependency removed
- tokio-rustls dependency removed

## macOS platform note
- **kqueue for TCP readiness**: On macOS, asupersync uses kqueue (not epoll) for TCP socket readiness notification (accept, read, write events). Verify that asupersync's reactor correctly uses kqueue for TcpListener and TcpStream on Darwin targets. If any platform-specific code exists for TCP handling, ensure `#[cfg(target_os = "macos")]` paths use kqueue and `#[cfg(target_os = "linux")]` paths use epoll. This is especially important for the web server's accept loop and the distributed module's TLS connections.

## Benchmark requirements
- **Criterion benchmarks for TLS handshake and throughput**: Add `benches/tcp_tls.rs` measuring:
  - TCP connection establishment latency (bind + connect + accept)
  - TLS handshake latency (client hello → finished) for TLS 1.2 and TLS 1.3
  - mTLS handshake latency (mutual certificate verification)
  - Sustained TCP throughput (MB/s) for streaming data over plain TCP
  - Sustained TLS throughput (MB/s) for streaming data over TLS
  - Comparison with tokio-rustls for equivalent TLS operations

## LabRuntime DPOR
- **Concurrent connection testing with DPOR**: Use LabRuntime schedule exploration (DPOR) to test concurrent TCP/TLS scenarios:
  - Multiple clients connecting to TcpListener simultaneously — verify all connections accepted, no drops
  - Interleaved TLS handshakes — verify no state corruption between concurrent handshakes
  - Race between connection accept and listener shutdown — verify clean teardown
  - Concurrent reads and writes on established TLS connections — verify no data corruption or reordering","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T03:49:05.178507Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T19:53:39.465924Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-1u55z","depends_on_id":"wa-2abzy","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T05:18:21.834643Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"},{"issue_id":"wa-1u55z","depends_on_id":"wa-1yz79","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T05:18:24.65586Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"}]}
 {"id":"wa-1u8k","title":"CLI: set pane priority at runtime","description":"## What
Add a command to set pane priority/weights without restarting.

## Why
Operators need to react to incidents in real time.

## How

 {"id":"wa-1wg","title":"[EPIC] Backup & Restore: Database Export, Import, Scheduled Backups","description":"# [EPIC] Backup & Restore System

## Mission
Provide users with confidence that their terminal history and configuration will never be lost, and make migration between machines or versions painless.

## Why This Matters
wa accumulates valuable data:
- Terminal transcripts (searchable history)
- Event timeline (debugging context)
- Configuration (tuned patterns, workflows)

Loss of this data would be a significant trust violation. Users need:
- Peace of mind that data is safe
- Easy migration to new machines
- Recovery from corruption or mistakes

## Components

### 1. Database Export (`wa backup export`)
Export the entire database to a portable format:
```bash
$ wa backup export --output backup_2026-01-18.wa
Creating backup...
  Segments: 12,345
  Events: 567
  Patterns: 23
  Config: included
Backup saved: backup_2026-01-18.wa (45 MB)
SHA256: abc123...
```

Export format:
- Self-describing archive (version header + manifest)
- SQLite dump + JSON metadata
- Optional compression (zstd)
- Integrity checksum

### 2. Database Import (`wa backup import`)
Restore from a backup:
```bash
$ wa backup import backup_2026-01-18.wa --verify
Verifying backup integrity... OK
Backup version: 1.2.0 (compatible)
Contents:
  Segments: 12,345
  Events: 567
  Config: included

Restore options:
  [1] Full restore (replace current data)
  [2] Merge (keep newer, import older)
  [3] Dry-run (show what would change)
```

Safety:
- Require explicit confirmation for full restore
- Create automatic backup before import
- Validate schema compatibility

### 3. Incremental Backups
For users who want continuous protection:
```toml
[backup]
enabled = true
schedule = "daily"  # or "hourly", "weekly"
retention_days = 30
destination = "~/.local/share/wa/backups/"
max_backups = 10
```

### 4. Configuration Export/Import
Separate from data, for sharing configs:
```bash
$ wa config export --output my-config.toml
$ wa config import my-config.toml --merge
```

### 5. Migration Assistant
When upgrading wa with breaking changes:
```bash
$ wa upgrade --from 1.x
Detected wa 1.x database
Migration required for:
  - Schema v1 → v2 (new event columns)
  - Config format update

Proceed? [y/N]
```

## Success Criteria
A user should be able to:
- create a backup file that is self-contained and verifiable (manifest + checksums)
- restore safely with a preview/dry-run path before any destructive change
- migrate between machines without manual file spelunking

Operational expectations (v0):
- backups are deterministic and explainable (stable manifest, clear versions)
- backups do not leak secrets (config redaction + no raw transcript dumping)
- imports refuse unsafe/incompatible inputs with actionable errors
- scheduled backups are robust (bounded disk usage, retention policy enforced)

## Testing
- Unit tests:
  - Export/import round-trip preserves all data
  - Version detection works across schema versions
  - Merge logic handles conflicts correctly

- Integration tests:
  - Backup with active watcher (snapshot isolation)
  - Import into empty vs populated database
  - Scheduled backup runs correctly

- E2E tests:
  - Full backup/restore cycle with real data
  - Migration from mock old version
  - Corrupt backup rejected with clear error

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] wa backup export creates portable backup files
- [ ] wa backup import restores with verification
- [ ] Incremental backup scheduling works
- [ ] wa config export/import for config-only migration
- [ ] Version migration assistant for upgrades
- [ ] All backups include integrity checksums
- [ ] Clear error messages for incompatible backups","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-01-18T19:55:22.915697374Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-29T02:58:36.963666064Z","closed_at":"2026-01-29T02:58:36.963516365Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-1wg","depends_on_id":"wa-37x","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:24Z","created_by":"import"}]}

 {"id":"wa-5ap","title":"Notification history: persistent log with queries, retry, and retention","description":"# Notification History: Persistent log of sent notifications

## Purpose
Track all sent notifications for debugging, auditing, and user review.

## Implementation

### Database Schema
```sql
CREATE TABLE notification_history (
    id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
    timestamp TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT (datetime('now')),

    -- Event reference
    event_id INTEGER REFERENCES events(id),

    -- Notification details
    channel TEXT NOT NULL,  -- 'webhook', 'desktop', 'slack', etc.
    title TEXT NOT NULL,
    body TEXT NOT NULL,
    severity TEXT NOT NULL,  -- 'info', 'warning', 'error', 'critical'

    -- Delivery status
    status TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'pending',  -- 'pending', 'sent', 'failed', 'throttled'
    error_message TEXT,

    -- Response tracking
    acknowledged_at TEXT,
    acknowledged_by TEXT,
    action_taken TEXT,

    -- Metadata
    metadata TEXT  -- JSON blob for channel-specific data
);

CREATE INDEX idx_notification_history_timestamp ON notification_history(timestamp);
CREATE INDEX idx_notification_history_status ON notification_history(status);
CREATE INDEX idx_notification_history_event ON notification_history(event_id);
```

### CLI Commands
```bash
# List recent notifications
$ wa notifications list
ID     Time       Channel   Event                        Status
-----  ---------  --------  ---------------------------  -------
123    14:30:15   webhook   codex.usage_limit_reached    sent
122    14:28:03   desktop   session.compaction           sent
121    14:25:00   webhook   codex.session_summary        throttled

# Show notification details
$ wa notifications show 123

# Filter by status/channel
$ wa notifications list --status=failed

# Retry failed notifications

```

### Robot Mode

 {"id":"wa-5da3j","title":"Phase 3: Replace tokio network I/O with asupersync","description":"# Phase 3: Replace tokio network I/O

## Goal
Replace all tokio network I/O (Unix sockets, TCP, TLS) with asupersync networking primitives.

## Scope
- tokio::net::UnixStream/UnixListener → asupersync::net equivalents
- tokio::net::TcpListener/TcpStream → asupersync::net::TcpListener/TcpStream
- tokio-rustls → asupersync TLS (rustls-based)
- tokio::io::{AsyncReadExt, AsyncWriteExt, BufReader} → asupersync I/O traits
- reqwest → asupersync::http client

## Key architectural note
asupersync provides its own networking stack that does NOT depend on tokio. The I/O reactor abstracts over epoll/kqueue/io_uring. All network ops go through Cx capability context.

## Affected modules
- mux_client.rs — UnixStream (997 LOC, most critical)
- ipc.rs — UnixListener + UnixStream (~400 LOC)
- native_events.rs — UnixListener (~400 LOC)
- web.rs — TcpListener (feature-gated)
- distributed.rs — TLS/mTLS (feature-gated)

## Migration risk: HIGH
Network I/O is where most subtle bugs hide. The mux_client.rs PDU framing protocol over Unix sockets is especially delicate.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T03:48:48.625855Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T05:18:33.55915Z","closed_at":"2026-02-10T05:18:33.559135Z","close_reason":"Flattened: Phase 3 was a pure bottleneck with no concrete work. - Whether actions can be undone
- How to recover from mistakes

- **Recovery**: Know what can be undone
- **Debugging**: Trace action sequences

- Timeline of all wa actions
- Grouped by workflow/pane
- Undo status for each action

### Visualization
- `wa history` - action timeline
- `wa history --pane 0` - pane-specific history
- `wa history --undo-able` - only undoable actions

### Undo Capabilities
- Send actions: show what was sent (can't undo)
- Workflow states: can abort/resume
- File changes (if any): can restore

### Integration
- Works with audit trail
- Links to workflow step logs
- Shows in TUI

## Success Criteria
- Users can see full action history
- Undo status is clear
- Recovery paths documented

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Undo status displayed per action
- [ ] Grouping by workflow works
- [ ] Integration with audit trail
- [ ] Tests cover visualization

## Testing
- Unit tests for undoability classification.
- Integration tests for history query joins.
- E2E scenario captures history output and logs.

### 2. Add alt-screen Detection Scenario

New scenario: "alt_screen_detection"

Purpose: Verify wa detects alt-screen transitions via escape sequence parsing (not Lua).

Test flow:
\`\`\`bash
run_scenario_alt_screen_detection() {
    # 1. Start wa watch
    # 2. Spawn a pane
    # 3. Send ESC[?1049h (enter alt-screen) to pane
    # 4. Verify wa detects alt-screen = true
    # 5. Send ESC[?1049l (leave alt-screen)
    # 6. Verify wa detects alt-screen = false
}
\`\`\`

Implementation sketch:
\`\`\`bash
run_scenario_alt_screen_detection() {
    local scenario_dir="$1"
    local wait_timeout="${TEST_WAIT_TIMEOUT:-30}"
    
    log_step "Starting wa watch..."
    wa watch --foreground > "$scenario_dir/wa_watch.log" 2>&1 &
    local wa_pid=$!
    sleep 2
    
    log_step "Spawning test pane..."
    local pane_id=$(wezterm cli spawn --cwd /tmp -- bash)
    sleep 1
    
    log_step "Verifying initial state (not alt-screen)..."
    local state=$(wa robot state --format json)
    local is_alt=$(echo "$state" | jq -r ".data.panes[] | select(.pane_id == $pane_id) | .is_alt_screen")
    if [[ "$is_alt" != "false" ]]; then
        log_fail "Expected is_alt_screen=false initially, got: $is_alt"
        return 1
    fi
    
    log_step "Sending alt-screen enter sequence..."
    # ESC [ ? 1049 h
    printf '\e[?1049h' | wezterm cli send-text --pane-id "$pane_id" --no-paste
    sleep 0.5  # Allow detection
    
    log_step "Verifying alt-screen detected..."
    state=$(wa robot state --format json)
    is_alt=$(echo "$state" | jq -r ".data.panes[] | select(.pane_id == $pane_id) | .is_alt_screen")
    if [[ "$is_alt" != "true" ]]; then
        log_fail "Expected is_alt_screen=true after ESC[?1049h, got: $is_alt"
        return 1
    fi
    
    log_step "Sending alt-screen leave sequence..."
    # ESC [ ? 1049 l
    printf '\e[?1049l' | wezterm cli send-text --pane-id "$pane_id" --no-paste
    sleep 0.5
    
    log_step "Verifying alt-screen exited..."
    state=$(wa robot state --format json)
    is_alt=$(echo "$state" | jq -r ".data.panes[] | select(.pane_id == $pane_id) | .is_alt_screen")
    if [[ "$is_alt" != "false" ]]; then
        log_fail "Expected is_alt_screen=false after ESC[?1049l, got: $is_alt"
        return 1
    fi
    
    log_pass "Alt-screen detection via escape sequences works!"
    cleanup_pane "$pane_id"
    kill "$wa_pid" 2>/dev/null
}
\`\`\`

### What Changed

In 0.2.0, we removed the Lua-based status update hook to dramatically improve WezTerm performance.

### Impact

- **No action required** for most users
- If you have custom integrations with \`wa event --from-status\`, see below

### Steps to Upgrade

1. **Update wa** to the latest version
2. **Re-run setup** to update your wezterm.lua:
   \`\`\`bash
   wa setup --wezterm
   \`\`\`
3. **Restart WezTerm** to pick up config changes

After upgrading, your wezterm.lua's WA-managed block should NOT contain:
- \`wezterm.on('update-status'\`

 {"id":"wa-oegrb.2.5","title":"Define capture-stage redaction policy and deterministic implementation boundaries","description":"## Goal
Specify and implement capture-stage redaction boundaries so sensitive tokens are protected without destroying search/replay utility.

## Background
A full recorder introduces privacy/security risk. Redaction policy must be explicit early, not bolted on later.

## Scope
- Define sensitive-pattern classes and redaction modes.
- Define where redaction is applied (before persist, before index, before query response).
- Define auditability for redaction actions.

## Deliverables
- Redaction policy and implementation requirements.
- Tests for deterministic redaction behavior and false-positive guardrails.

## Acceptance criteria
- Sensitive fields are consistently protected across storage/index/API layers.
- Redaction behavior is measurable and tunable.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"OrangeBarn","created_at":"2026-02-12T06:04:11.797785Z","created_by":"WildSpring","updated_at":"2026-02-12T08:08:47.317326Z","closed_at":"2026-02-12T08:08:47.317313Z","close_reason":"Capture-stage redaction policy and deterministic implementation boundaries documented with downstream-ready requirements","labels":["capture","privacy","redaction","security"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-oegrb.2.5","depends_on_id":"wa-oegrb.2","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-12T06:04:11.797785Z","created_by":"WildSpring","metadata":"{}"},{"issue_id":"wa-oegrb.2.5","depends_on_id":"wa-oegrb.2.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-12T06:04:11.987531Z","created_by":"WildSpring","metadata":"{}"}],"comments":[{"id":9,"issue_id":"wa-oegrb.2.5","author":"Jeff Emanuel","text":"Claimed by OrangeBarn.  {"id":"wa-oegrb.2.6","title":"Specify and validate capture backpressure/overflow behavior under swarm load","description":"## Goal
Define capture-path backpressure/failure semantics and verify behavior under overload so recorder does not destabilize core watcher operations.

## Background
Recorder capture must fail safely under stress; silent degradation is unacceptable.

## Scope
- Define queueing and overflow policy (drop/slow/spool) with rationale.
- Define telemetry and alerting conditions.
- Validate with overload and fault injection tests.

## Deliverables
- Backpressure policy doc and implementation plan.
- Overload test scenarios with expected outcomes.

## Acceptance criteria
- Overload behavior is deterministic and observable.
- Core watcher stability is preserved under recorder stress.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-12T06:04:11.831967Z","created_by":"WildSpring","updated_at":"2026-02-12T06:04:12.048598Z","labels":["backpressure","capture","reliability"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-oegrb.2.6","depends_on_id":"wa-oegrb.2","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-12T06:04:11.831967Z","created_by":"WildSpring","metadata":"{}"},{"issue_id":"wa-oegrb.2.6","depends_on_id":"wa-oegrb.2.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-12T06:04:12.018117Z","created_by":"WildSpring","metadata":"{}"},{"issue_id":"wa-oegrb.2.6","depends_on_id":"wa-oegrb.2.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-12T06:04:12.048557Z","created_by":"WildSpring","metadata":"{}"}]}
 {"id":"wa-oegrb.3","title":"Storage track: append-only writer path + backend abstraction + FrankenSQLite integration","description":"## Track objective
Build the append-only persistence path for flight-recorder events, including backend abstraction, high-throughput writer path, and FrankenSQLite integration path with safe fallback.

Logging quality is bounded by write-path reliability and throughput. This track ensures durability under load while preserving rollout safety.

## Deliverables
- Storage abstraction for recorder log backends
- Baseline backend (for immediate bring-up)
- FrankenSQLite backend plan/integration path
- Crash recovery/replay and corruption handling strategy
